name: Slovenia (Slovenija)
summary: Slovenia is a civil-law unitary republic whose family-law framework operates under the Družinski zakonik (Family Code) of 2017 (Law DZ-1) in force 15 April 2019. Parental care (starševska skrb) is governed by Družinski zakonik arts. 138-181; joint exercise of parental care during marriage is the statutory default. The Vrhovno sodišče (Supreme Court of the Republic of Slovenia, Ljubljana) is the apex civil court; the Ustavno sodišče (Constitutional Court) operates separate constitutional-review and individual-constitutional-complaint jurisdiction. Psychology profession is regulated under the Slovenian framework with Društvo psihologov Slovenije (DPS) operating peak-body ethics oversight. Slovenia is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label; courts operate substantively under the otrokova korist (child's benefit / best-interests) standard. As an EU Member State and Council of Europe member, Slovenia operates within the Strasbourg Article 8 positive-obligations framework.

statutory_framework.title: Family Code 2017 — Parental care
statutory_framework.relevance: Federal statute on family law adopted 21 March 2017, in force 15 April 2019. Substantial modernisation of Slovenian family law including parental care framework. Joint exercise during marriage is the statutory default; otrokova korist welfare standard codified.

apex_courts.name: Vrhovno sodišče Republike Slovenije (Supreme Court of the Republic of Slovenia)
apex_courts.role: Apex civil court. Family-law decisions reach the Supreme Court via the Višje sodišče (Higher Court of Appeal) following first-instance Okrožno sodišče (Circuit Court) determinations.

apex_courts.name: Ustavno sodišče (Constitutional Court)
apex_courts.role: Constitutional Court with original jurisdiction over constitutional review and individual constitutional complaints.

professional_regulators.name: Društvo psihologov Slovenije (DPS)
professional_regulators.role: Peak professional association for psychologists in Slovenia. Operates ethics code and complaints procedure.

anonymisation_convention: Slovenian family-law decisions are anonymised per Vrhovno sodišče practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Slovenia independence + Constitution 1991 + UNCRC succession
key_developments.description: Slovenia declared independence 25 June 1991 from SFR Yugoslavia — first Yugoslav republic to declare independence following Ten-Day War (27 June – 7 July 1991). Constitution of the Republic of Slovenia adopted 23 December 1991 — establishing parliamentary republic framework with Art. 53 codifying family-protection-clauses and Art. 56 children's-rights provisions. UNCRC succession 6 July 1992 (succession to earlier SFR Yugoslavia 1991 accession).

key_developments.title: Council of Europe membership + ECHR ratification
key_developments.description: Slovenia joined the Council of Europe 14 May 1993 — ECHR ratified 28 June 1994 effective. ECHR engagement subsequently became a major source of family-law jurisprudence including Article 8 (right to respect for family life) and Article 6 (fair trial) decisions affecting custody and visitation matters.

key_developments.title: Hague Convention 1980 accession
key_developments.description: Slovenia acceded to the Hague Convention on the Civil Aspects of International Child Abduction 1980 effective 1 June 1994 — among the earliest post-Yugoslav Hague accessions. Hague-1980 framework substantially significant for cross-border-displacement custody matters.

key_developments.title: European Union accession + EU acquis communautaire integration + NATO membership
key_developments.description: Slovenia joined the European Union 1 May 2004 — first Yugoslav successor state to join EU — substantially integrated EU acquis communautaire including Brussels IIa Regulation (now Brussels IIb 2019/1111). Schengen Area 2007, Eurozone 2007 (first post-Communist Eurozone state). Slovenia joined NATO 29 March 2004.

key_developments.title: Registration of Same-Sex Partnerships Act (early Slovenian same-sex partnership framework)
key_developments.description: Registration of Same-Sex Partnerships Act (Zakon o registraciji istospolne partnerske skupnosti) enacted 22 June 2005, in force 23 July 2006 — among the earliest same-sex registered partnership frameworks in CEE/Yugoslav successor states.

key_developments.title: Constitutional referendum + same-sex marriage framework debates
key_developments.description: Constitutional Court ruled 2015 on same-sex marriage framework. Subsequent referendum 20 December 2015 substantially affected legal-rights trajectory. Constitutional Court 8 July 2022 decision struck down restrictive provisions enabling same-sex marriage equality.

key_developments.title: Družinski zakonik adopted (Family Code Law DZ-1)
key_developments.description: New Family Code adopted 21 March 2017 — substantively modernising family-law replacing 1976 Marriage and Family Relations Act. Among the more recent CEE civil-code family-law modernisations.

key_developments.title: Družinski zakonik in force
key_developments.description: Družinski zakonik 2017 in force 15 April 2019 — substantial modernisation of Slovenian family law including parental-care framework. Arts. 138-181 govern parental care with joint exercise during marriage as statutory default; otrokova korist welfare standard codified.

key_developments.title: Constitutional Court same-sex marriage decision + same-sex marriage effective
key_developments.description: Constitutional Court of Slovenia decision 8 July 2022 struck down restrictive provisions, enabling same-sex marriage equality and joint adoption — first Yugoslav successor state to legalise same-sex marriage. Substantive amendment to Družinski zakonik.

key_developments.title: Družinski zakonik 2017 + 2019 implementation jurisprudence + Varuh človekovih pravic position
key_developments.description: Družinski zakonik (Family Code) 2017 effective 15 April 2019 implementation jurisprudence development 2020-2023 — multiple Vrhovno sodišče decisions interpreting modernised parental-responsibility framework under arts. 138-181. Varuh človekovih pravic (Ombudsperson for Human Rights) and Zagovornik otrokovih pravic (Children's Rights Advocate) institutional positions addressing PA-related issues. Substantively significant post-Družinski-zakonik jurisprudential development.

key_developments.title: Vrhovno sodišče + Ustavno sodišče — otrokova korist substantive register
key_developments.description: Vrhovno sodišče and Ustavno sodišče continue to develop otrokova korist (child's benefit / best-interests) jurisprudence under Družinski zakonik arts. 138-181 + Constitution Art. 53-56 + ECHR Article 8 framework in custody disputes without adopting the 'parental alienation' label as a doctrinal term.

key_developments.title: Slovenia recognition of Palestine + UN Security Council non-permanent membership 2024-2025 + family-law-international context framework
key_developments.description: Slovenia recognition of State of Palestine 4 June 2024 (with Ireland + Spain + Norway) + Slovenia UN Security Council non-permanent membership 2024-2025 framework + substantively distinctive globally first wave of EU-state recognition of Palestine in 2024 + structurally distinctive Slovenia post-Yugoslav-secession-1991 framework operating sustained international-recognition-foreign-policy framework + substantively significant institutional context affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory and family-law-implementation under Družinski zakonik 2017 framework + Hague Convention 1980 cross-border implementation framework + otrokova korist substantive register evolution within EU Brussels IIb framework + Slovenia EU Presidency-of-the-Council 2008 + 2021 legacy framework.

structural_findings: Slovenia sits structurally within the CEE/Balkan civil-law cluster alongside Croatia + Bulgaria + Romania + Serbia — modernised Family Code 2017 + welfare-standard family-court framework + Constitutional Court individual-complaint jurisdiction.

Družinski zakonik 2017 represents one of the more recent civil-code family-law modernisations within the corpus — adopted 2017 and in force 2019. Structurally adjacent to the broader EU civil-law cluster.
Psychology profession regulation operates through DPS peak-body ethics oversight.

sources.title: PIS — Pravno-informacijski sistem
sources.publisher: Government of the Republic of Slovenia

editorial_notes: Slovenia jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-08 from 2 to 10 key_developments with full independence-to-contemporary trajectory: 1991-Slovenia-independence-first-Yugoslav-republic-+-Constitution-+-UNCRC-succession + 1993-Council-of-Europe-membership-+-1994-ECHR-ratification + 1994-Hague-1980-accession + 2004-EU-accession-first-Yugoslav-+-NATO-membership + 2006-Registration-of-Same-Sex-Partnerships-Act + 2015-Constitutional-referendum-+-same-sex-marriage-framework-debates + 2017-Družinski-zakonik-adopted + 2019-Družinski-zakonik-in-force + 2022-Constitutional-Court-same-sex-marriage-decision-first-Yugoslav-successor + 2024-Vrhovno-sodišče-Ustavno-sodišče-otrokova-korist.

CEE/Balkan civil-law framework (Družinski zakonik 2017 in force 2019 + Constitution 1991 + Constitutional Court individual-complaint + DPS peak-body psychology regulation + Hague Convention 1980 + EU acquis + Council of Europe/ECHR + NATO).

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive otrokova korist analysis under Družinski zakonik arts. 138-181 + Constitution + ECHR Article 8 framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins CEE-Balkan-civil-law-cluster + post-Yugoslav-civil-law (first Yugoslav successor state to declare independence) + Council-of-Europe-ECHR-Strasbourg-adjacent + Hague-1980-Western-Balkans-earliest + first-Yugoslav-successor-EU-member-2004 + first-Yugoslav-successor-Eurozone-2007 + first-Yugoslav-successor-same-sex-marriage-2022 + deeply-EU-integrated-post-Yugoslav clusters within the corpus.
